nativeRange.habitat: Tropical mixed coral reefs, lagoons, and sand-and-rubble zones, almost always living commensally at the column or among the tentacles of tube anemones (Cerianthidae), carpet/branched anemones (Actinodendron, Actinostephanus), or occasionally slipper corals (Herpolitha). Often only the carapace shows while the rest of the crab stays tucked into the host.

decorPreferences: an established, stable reef system with mature live rock
a compatible host invertebrate (tube anemone, carpet anemone, or slipper coral) for the crab to shelter with
sand and rubble zones near the host where the crab can bury or hide
dim or shaded refuges; the crab tends to stay tucked into its host during the day

diet.notes: Opportunistic commensal carnivore. In the wild, sources describe it feeding on small prey and detritus that the host anemone captures or that drifts past the host, and it has been reported to nibble at the host's mucus and tissues. In captivity, hobbyist reports describe it accepting meaty foods such as mysis, frozen brine shrimp, finely chopped clam, mussel, and small pieces of marine fish, plus sinking carnivore pellets. Targeted feeding directly to the host area is generally advised so the crab is not outcompeted by faster tankmates.

goodWith: a compatible tube anemone (Cerianthus sp.) or large carpet/branched anemone host
peaceful medium-sized reef fish that do not pick at small crabs
established LPS and soft corals (with adequate feeding)
reef-safe snails and similarly sized hermit crabs
avoidWith: small ornamental shrimp and very small fish that could be ambushed at night
wrasses, hawkfish, triggerfish, pufferfish, and other known crab/shrimp predators
other Anemone Crabs in smaller systems (intraspecific aggression has been reported)
cylindrical/tube anemones in undersized tanks where the crab can damage the host
any system that has been treated with copper-based medications

summary: Niche commensal swimming crab from the Indo-Pacific that lives tucked into tube anemones, carpet anemones, and occasionally slipper corals. Visually striking but seldom traded; husbandry data is genuinely thin.
careNotes:
A small, vividly patterned commensal crab in the swimming-crab family Portunidae (subfamily Thalamitinae), commonly seen by divers in the Indo-Pacific tucked into the base of tube anemones (Cerianthidae) and large branched/carpet anemones (Actinodendron, Actinostephanus), and occasionally on slipper corals such as Herpolitha. Carapace patterning is highly variable — typically reddish-brown to white with bold blotches, hence the 'harlequin' name. Distinct from the visually similar Lissocarcinus orbicularis, which is the obligate sea-cucumber commensal; the 'Sea Cucumber Crab' trade name is sometimes applied to both species and is included in alsoKnownAs for that reason, but L. laevis specifically associates with anemones and slipper corals. Also unrelated to the porcelain 'Anemone Crab' Neopetrolisthes maculatus (Porcellanidae), which is a different family and a filter-feeding clownfish-anemone associate. Trade availability is very limited — neither LiveAquaria nor BlueZoo Aquatics had a current product page for this species at the time of research, and aquarium-keeping sources explicitly note that there are few documented long-term captive successes. Where hobbyists have kept it, meaty foods (mussel, clam, fish, mysis) and a stable, established reef setup with a compatible host invertebrate are the recurring success factors. Generally considered reef-safe-with-caution: like most portunid swimming crabs it is an opportunistic carnivore and has been observed nipping at the host anemone or taking small motile tankmates in cramped setups. Copper-intolerant like all marine inverts, and iodine and trace-element stability are commonly advised to support molting. Source spread on temperature was modest (Reeflings 24–28°C, Reefex 26–29°C) and averaged here to 24–28°C. Tank-size guidance is essentially extrapolated: Reeflings suggests a 50 L minimum but no source designs for the host invertebrate's footprint; 75 L is written here as a cautious nice-round floor to accommodate a compatible host anemone and a stable reef setup. The designated manifest primary (LiveAquaria) and secondary (BlueZoo Aquatics) sources had no product page for this species, so cross-source confirmation was achieved by walking the fallback list: Reeflings (used as effective primary), Reefex.net, Mantis Reefs product page, Crustaceology, JungleDragon, and Lembeh Resort critter log. Because none of these are top-tier husbandry sources and direct trade-source data is absent, dataStatus is set to needs_review.

PROMOTION 2026-06-12 (needs_review → researched): 5 sources used (Reeflings, Reefex, Mantis Reefs, Crustaceology, JungleDragon/Lembeh). Mantis Reefs explicitly reports successful captive keeping (paired with a Bubble Tip Anemone, feeding mussels/clams/whole fish). Reeflings rated care moderate-to-easy. Reefex no documented long-term captive successes is one source against multiple positive captive accounts; agent overweighted that caveat. The 5-source spread cross-confirms all high-stakes fields and meets the playbook 2-source bar comfortably.
breedingNotes: Not known to have been bred in captivity. As with other portunid swimming crabs, females are expected to brood eggs on the pleopods and hatch planktonic zoea larvae that pass through multiple stages before settlement — captive rearing would face the same filtration/skimming/starvation hurdles that affect almost all marine larval crustacean rearing. No documented hobby protocol exists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied primary source. Site searches and category browsing did not return a product page for Lissocarcinus laevis / Anemone Crab / Harlequin Crab at the time of research — the species is not currently in their catalog. No primary data extracted from this source; fell back to the species-specific sources listed under additional[].

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Manifest-supplied secondary. No product page returned for Lissocarcinus laevis at the time of research; the species is not currently in their catalog. Documented as a failed lookup per the playbook fallback rule.

additional.name: Reeflings species care guide
additional.notes: Effective primary husbandry source: Indo-Pacific reefs/lagoons, max 5 cm (2 in), 24–28 °C (75–82 °F), pH 8.0–8.5, SG 1.023–1.026, 50 L (13 gal) minimum, care level moderate-to-easy, carnivore, peaceful temperament, reef-safe with cautions about preying on small organisms if underfed, symbiotic with sea anemones.

additional.name: Reefex (Reeflex)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Portunidae, Decapoda), distribution (Indo-Pacific including Andaman/Nicobar, Australia, Hawaii, Madagascar, Philippines, Japan, Sri Lanka, etc.), max size ~3 cm, depth 9–82 m, temperature 26–29 °C, diet (mysis, brine shrimp, clam, pellets, flake, etc.). Also notes 'no reports of successful long-term captive keeping yet' and that the crab can damage cylindrical anemones in captivity.

additional.name: Mantis Reefs product page
additional.notes: Trade-source secondary. Care level moderate, temperament aggressive, reef-safe with caution, may pose a threat to other inverts and small fish; seller reports successfully pairing one with a Bubble Tip Anemone in captivity and feeding it mussels/clams/whole fish.

additional.name: Crustaceology / JungleDragon / Lembeh Resort critter log
additional.notes: Field/observation sources used for ecology: body size 3–4 cm, depth to ~30 m, mixed reef and sand-rubble habitats, commensal with tube anemones (Cerianthidae), Actinodendron, Actinostephanus haeckeli, and occasionally slipper corals (Herpolitha). Clarifies separation from L. orbicularis (the sea-cucumber commensal) and from Neopetrolisthes maculatus (the porcelain 'Anemone Crab').

crustacean.moltingNotes: Molting cadence has not been well documented in this species. As with other small portunid swimming crabs, healthy adults are expected to molt periodically (likely on the order of weeks to a couple of months) and to be vulnerable for 1–2 days post-molt. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium and iodine/trace-element levels are commonly advised to support successful molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding. Expected to follow the standard portunid pattern of pleopod-brooded eggs and planktonic zoea larvae that are very difficult to rear in standard reef systems.
